explicit_text: I was born in Columbus, Georgia, on December 5th, 1897. It was a small town where my family lived modestly, and my father worked as a coppersmith for the railroad. Growing up there, none of my family had any connection to writing, so it was quite surprising that I developed an interest in becoming a newspaperman.

Growing up there, none of my family had any connection to writing, so it was quite surprising that I developed an interest in becoming a newspaperman.

explicit_text: Dwight D. Eisenhower once said something that really resonated with me about my childhood: 'I suppose we were poor, but we didn't know it.' That perfectly describes my early years in Columbus, Georgia, where despite my father's modest income, we never felt deprived. His words capture the spirit of those times better than I ever could.

His words capture the spirit of those times better than I ever could.

explicit_text: My father worked at the Central of Georgia Railway as a coppersmith, which was a steady but humble job. This connection to the railroad was a big part of my early life in Columbus, Georgia. Although I never worked there myself, the railway was part of the fabric of our community and my family's livelihood.

explicit_text: I was inspired to become a newspaperman after seeing a picture of Richard Harding Davis dressed as a war correspondent. explicit_text: Frank Mankiewicz was involved in a family situation about twenty years ago when his sister Joanna fell in love with a man everyone in New York opposed. Frank was pragmatic and said that since Joanna was the only one marrying him, she was the only one who needed to be pleased. I remember that story well because it showed Frank's supportive nature.
